content:
# Exodus Chapter 36 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 107-108; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 79-80 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_036_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_036_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_036_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_036_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Craftsmen Begin and the Surplus of Offerings (vv. 1-7)

**1** And Bezalel and Oholiab shall work, and every man wise of heart in whom YHWH has put wisdom and understanding in them, to know how to do all the work of the service of the holy place, according to all that YHWH commanded.

**2** And Moses called to Bezalel and to Oholiab and to every man wise of heart in whose heart YHWH had put wisdom -- everyone whose heart lifted him to draw near to the work to do it.

**3** And they received from before Moses all the contribution which the Sons of Israel had brought for the work of the service of the holy place to do it. And they -- they kept bringing to him a freewill-offering morning by morning.

**4** And all the wise ones came -- the ones doing all the work of the holy place -- each man from his work which they were doing --

**5** and they said to Moses, saying, "The people are bringing more than enough for the service of the work which YHWH commanded to do."

**6** And Moses commanded, and they caused a voice to pass through the camp, saying, "Let no man or woman do any more work for the contribution of the holy place." And the people were restrained from bringing.

**7** And the work was sufficient for them -- for all the work -- to do it, and more than enough.

---

## The Ten Curtains of the Tabernacle (vv. 8-13)

**8** And every wise-hearted one among the doers of the work made the tabernacle: ten curtains of fine twisted linen and blue and purple and scarlet of worm -- cherubim, the work of a designer, he made them.

**9** The length of the one curtain -- twenty-eight by the cubit, and the width -- four by the cubit, the one curtain; one measure for all the curtains.

**10** And he joined five of the curtains one to another, and five curtains he joined one to another.

**11** And he made loops of blue on the edge of the one curtain at the end of the joining; likewise he made on the edge of the outermost curtain in the second joining.

**12** Fifty loops he made on the one curtain, and fifty loops he made on the end of the curtain which was in the second joining -- the loops corresponding one to another.

**13** And he made fifty clasps of gold, and he joined the curtains one to another with the clasps; and the tabernacle became one.

---

## The Goat-Hair Curtains (vv. 14-18)

**14** And he made curtains of goat-hair for a tent over the tabernacle -- eleven curtains he made them.

**15** The length of the one curtain -- thirty by the cubit, and four cubits the width of the one curtain; one measure for the eleven curtains.

**16** And he joined five of the curtains by themselves, and six of the curtains by themselves.

**17** And he made fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain in the joining, and fifty loops he made on the edge of the curtain of the second joining.

**18** And he made fifty clasps of bronze to join the tent, to be one.

---

## The Coverings and the Boards (vv. 19-30)

**19** And he made a covering for the tent -- skins of rams dyed red, and a covering of skins of takhash from above.

**20** And he made the boards for the tabernacle of acacia wood, standing upright.

**21** Ten cubits the length of the board, and a cubit and a half of the cubit the width of the one board.

**22** Two tenons for the one board, joined one to another; thus he made for all the boards of the tabernacle.

**23** And he made the boards for the tabernacle -- twenty boards for the south side, southward.

**24** And forty bases of silver he made under the twenty boards -- two bases under the one board for its two tenons, and two bases under the one board for its two tenons.

**25** And for the second side of the tabernacle, for the north side, he made twenty boards,

**26** and their forty bases of silver -- two bases under the one board and two bases under the one board.

**27** And for the rear of the tabernacle, westward, he made six boards.

**28** And two boards he made for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear.

**29** And they were twinned from below, and together they were complete at its top, to the one ring; thus he made for both of them, for the two corners.

**30** And there were eight boards, and their bases of silver -- sixteen bases -- two bases, two bases under the one board.

---

## The Bars (vv. 31-34)

**31** And he made bars of acacia wood -- five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle,

**32** and five bars for the boards of the second side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the boards of the tabernacle for the rear, westward.

**33** And he made the middle bar to pass through in the midst of the boards from end to end.

**34** And the boards he overlaid with gold, and their rings he made of gold -- holders for the bars; and the bars he overlaid with gold.

---

## The Veil and the Screen (vv. 35-38)

**35** And he made the veil of blue and purple and scarlet of worm and fine twisted linen -- the work of a designer he made it -- cherubim.

**36** And he made for it four pillars of acacia, and he overlaid them with gold -- their hooks of gold -- and he cast for them four bases of silver.

**37** And he made a screen for the entrance of the tent of blue and purple and scarlet of worm and fine twisted linen -- the work of an embroiderer.

**38** And its pillars -- five -- and their hooks; and he overlaid their tops and their bands with gold; and their bases -- five -- of bronze.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 36 across pages 107-108, continuing directly from the appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab at the close of chapter 35. No parashah petuhah separates 35:35 from 36:1; the narrative flows without break from appointment to execution, confirming the literary unity of 35:30-36:7 as a single pericope. A setumah (closed paragraph) break appears between v. 7 and v. 8, marking the transition from the surplus-offering narrative to the construction account. The three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation is maintained throughout. A masorah parva note is visible beside the rare Niphal vayyikkale ("were restrained") in v. 6. The distinctive distributive babboqer babboqer ("morning by morning") in v. 3 carries its own cantillation pattern. The detailed construction account (vv. 8-38) occupies the bulk of folio 108, reproducing with near-verbatim fidelity the instructions of chapter 26 -- now shifted from the imperative "you shall make" to the narrative "he made."

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 79-80) presents what is perhaps the most significant MT-LXX divergence in the entire Pentateuch. The manuscript pages are headed CAP. XXVI and CAP. XXVII (visible in the images), confirming that the LXX dramatically rearranges the tabernacle construction material. What MT presents as Exodus 36 (the execution of the construction) is relocated in the LXX to correspond with the original instruction chapters (MT ch. 26). The LXX essentially merges instruction and execution, whereas the MT preserves the full command-then-fulfillment structure as a literary diptych. The surplus-offering narrative (MT 36:1-7) is present in the LXX but positioned differently in the sequence. The Vaticanus text on pages 79-80 covers the curtains, clasps, boards, and bars in a sequence aligned with chapter 26's instructions. The three-column uncial scriptio continua format is maintained. Scholars debate whether the LXX reflects a shorter Hebrew Vorlage or an editorial abbreviation; SOPHIA follows the MT arrangement throughout, as it preserves the theologically significant command-fulfillment architecture.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 36 divides into two distinct movements, linked by a single theological claim: YHWH's dwelling is built by willing hearts and wise hands, and the result is superabundance.

**The Surplus of Generosity (vv. 1-7):** This pericope is unique in the Torah -- nowhere else must the people be restrained from giving. The seven-verse unit moves from commission (v. 1) through summoning (v. 2) to the astonishing surplus (vv. 3-7). The phrase babboqer babboqer ("morning by morning," v. 3) uses distributive repetition to indicate the relentless, ongoing nature of the giving -- each and every morning, without fail. The craftsmen must leave their own work to report the problem (v. 4), using the emphatic distributive ish-ish ("each man, each man"). Moses then causes a proclamation to "pass through" (ya'aviru qol) the camp -- the same Hiphil of avar used for public edicts -- commanding cessation. The Niphal vayyikkale ("were restrained") in v. 6 indicates that the people's generosity was not self-limiting but required external force to halt. The final verse (v. 7) stands as a strikingly terse summary: the work was dayam ("sufficient") and vehoter ("surplus") -- not merely enough but overflowing. This surplus reverses the golden calf economy: in chapter 32 the people stripped their gold for an idol; here they give so lavishly for YHWH's dwelling that they must be forcibly stopped.

**The Command-Fulfillment Construction (vv. 8-38):** The construction account mirrors Exodus 26 with near-verbatim fidelity, systematically shifting from second-person imperative to third-person narrative. This repetition is not redundant but liturgical -- like a responsive reading where the congregation's reply confirms reception of the officiant's words. Every dimension, material, and structural detail echoes the original command, forming the right panel of a literary diptych whose left panel is chapter 26.

The phrase vayehi ha-mishkan ekhad ("and the tabernacle became one," v. 13) carries theological weight beyond architectural description. The diverse materials (linen, dyed wool, gold, silver, bronze, acacia, animal skins) and the diverse workers (Bezalel, Oholiab, wise-hearted men and women) are unified into a single dwelling. The ekhad ("one") of the tabernacle echoes the ekhad of YHWH -- the God who is one dwells in a structure that is one.

The graded holiness of the materials is carefully preserved: inner curtains are fine linen with cherubim (ma'aseh khoshev, "work of a designer"); outer curtains are goat-hair without decoration. The inner veil bears cherubim on silver bases; the entrance screen bears embroidery on bronze bases. Gold yields to silver yields to bronze; designer's work yields to embroiderer's work; figured fabric yields to plain. Each transition marks a step away from the innermost sanctum. This spatial theology -- increasing purity and costliness as one moves toward YHWH's presence -- is the architectural expression of the Priestly understanding that proximity to the Holy demands corresponding elevation of material and craft.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Shall work" for ve'asah (v. 1):** EMET renders "shall work," LOGOS "shall work," PNEUMA "set to work." SOPHIA adopts "shall work" (EMET/LOGOS) because the waw-consecutive perfect continues the discourse from 35:35, carrying forward YHWH's command. PNEUMA's "set to work" captures the sense but loses the specific verb form.

2. **"Whose heart lifted him" for nesa'o libbo (v. 2):** Consistent with the decision in Exodus 35:21, SOPHIA renders nasa as "lifted" rather than "stirred" (LOGOS) or "moved" (PNEUMA). The verb is physical and directional -- an upward carrying -- not a generic internal agitation. See EXO_035 Disagreement #1 for full discussion.

3. **"They received" for vayyiqkhu (v. 3):** EMET renders "they took," LOGOS "they received," PNEUMA "they received." SOPHIA adopts "they received" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) because the context describes the craftsmen accepting the contributed materials from Moses' custody. "Took" (EMET) is the literal rendering of laqakh but could suggest unauthorized acquisition. "Received" better captures the transfer of entrusted materials. The LXX's elabon ("they took/received") supports either reading.

4. **"Morning by morning" for babboqer babboqer (v. 3):** EMET renders "morning by morning," LOGOS "morning by morning," PNEUMA "morning after morning." SOPHIA adopts "morning by morning" (EMET/LOGOS) because the distributive repetition of the bare noun (boqer boqer) should be mirrored in English with the simplest possible preposition. "After" (PNEUMA) implies sequential progression, whereas the Hebrew's repetition emphasizes each morning individually, without reference to the previous one.

5. **"Each man from his work" for ish-ish mi-melakhto (v. 4):** EMET renders "each man from his work," LOGOS "each man from his work," PNEUMA "left what they were working on." SOPHIA adopts "each man from his work" (EMET/LOGOS) because the emphatic distributive ish-ish ("man, man") should be preserved -- it emphasizes individuality. PNEUMA paraphrases the construction entirely, converting the Hebrew's compact phrase into a verbal clause.

6. **"More than enough" for marbim le-havi mi-dei (v. 5):** EMET renders "are bringing more than enough," LOGOS "are bringing more than enough," PNEUMA "are bringing far more than is needed." SOPHIA adopts "are bringing more than enough" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hiphil participle marbim ("are multiplying") modifies the infinitive le-havi ("to bring"), and mi-dei serves as a comparative marker. PNEUMA's "far more than is needed" adds the intensifier "far" and substitutes "needed" for "enough" -- both interpretive additions.

7. **"Caused a voice to pass" for vayya'aviru qol (v. 6):** EMET renders "caused a voice to pass," LOGOS "caused a proclamation to pass," PNEUMA "sent this announcement." SOPHIA adopts "caused a voice to pass" (EMET) because qol means "voice" (not "proclamation" or "announcement"), and the Hiphil of avar ("to cause to pass") should be rendered concretely. LOGOS's "proclamation" is an interpretive upgrading of the simple "voice." PNEUMA's "sent this announcement" restructures entirely.

8. **"Were restrained" for vayyikkale (v. 6):** All three translators render the Niphal of kala as "restrained." SOPHIA concurs. The passive form is theologically significant: the people's generosity required external force to halt. PNEUMA's additional "from bringing more" adds "more" where the Hebrew has only me-havi ("from bringing") -- but this is a minor clarification consistent with the context.

9. **"And more than enough" for vehoter (v. 7):** EMET renders "and more -- (surplus)," LOGOS "and more than enough," PNEUMA "and then some." SOPHIA adopts "and more than enough" (LOGOS) because it captures the Hiphil participle of yatar ("to be left over, to have surplus") in idiomatic English without EMET's parenthetical gloss or PNEUMA's colloquialism. The word stands as an emphatic coda -- a single-word theological statement of overflow.

10. **"Fine twisted linen" for shesh moshezar (v. 8):** EMET renders "twisted fine linen," LOGOS "fine twisted linen," PNEUMA "finely twisted linen." SOPHIA adopts "fine twisted linen" (LOGOS) as the most natural English word order. "Twisted" renders the Hophal participle of shazar ("to twist/twine"), indicating the linen was twined or plied. PNEUMA's "finely" adds an adverb not present in the Hebrew.

11. **"Designer" for khoshev (vv. 8, 35):** All three translators render khoshev as "designer" (EMET/LOGOS) or "master designer" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA adopts "designer" without "master" because the Hebrew khoshev means "thinker/deviser" -- the skill grade is indicated by the construction (ma'aseh khoshev, "work of a designer"), not by an added intensifier. The LXX's huphantou ("weaver") is a different craft designation.

12. **"Boards" for qerashim (vv. 20-34):** EMET and LOGOS render "boards," PNEUMA renders "frames." SOPHIA adopts "boards" consistent with the decision in EXO_035:11. The Hebrew qeresh traditionally means "board/plank." The scholarly proposal of "frame" (a latticed structure) is noted but not adopted, as it goes beyond standard lexical evidence.

13. **"Tenons" for yadot (v. 22):** All three translators render yadot as "tenons." SOPHIA concurs. The literal meaning is "hands" -- projections that grip the silver bases -- but "tenons" is the established architectural term for mortise-and-tenon joinery, which accurately describes the structural function. The LXX's agkoniskous ("small elbows/projections") confirms the projection-and-socket understanding.

14. **"Twinned" for to'amim (v. 29):** EMET renders "doubled," LOGOS "twinned," PNEUMA "joined together." SOPHIA adopts "twinned" (LOGOS) because the root ta'am means specifically "to be twin/doubled" -- the corner boards are paired like twins, not merely joined. PNEUMA's "joined together" is too generic, losing the specific twin-imagery. EMET's "doubled" is close but misses the organic pairing that "twinned" conveys.

15. **"Holders" for battim (v. 34):** EMET and LOGOS render "holders," PNEUMA omits the term. SOPHIA adopts "holders" because the Hebrew battim (literally "houses") describes the ring-settings through which the bars pass -- the rings serve as "houses" for the bars, the same term used for gem settings in the breastpiece (28:11). PNEUMA's omission loses this architectural metaphor.

16. **"Veil" for parokhet (v. 35):** All three translators render parokhet as "veil" or "inner curtain." SOPHIA adopts "veil" (EMET/LOGOS) as the standard English rendering for the interior curtain separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. PNEUMA's "inner curtain" is descriptive but loses the technical term. The LXX's to katapetasma ("the curtain/veil") -- the same term used in the NT for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51) -- confirms the traditional rendering.

17. **"Screen" for masakh (v. 37):** All three translators render masakh as "screen." SOPHIA concurs. The masakh is the outer entrance curtain, distinct from the inner parokhet. The distinction between the two -- different craft grades (embroiderer vs. designer), different materials (bronze bases vs. silver bases) -- preserves the Priestly system of graded holiness.

18. **"Bands" for khashukeihem (v. 38):** EMET and LOGOS render "bands," PNEUMA renders "bands." Full agreement. The Hebrew khashukeihem (from khashaq, "to desire/bind") designates the connecting bands or fillets encircling the pillars. The LXX's kephalides ("capitals") is a different architectural element.
